evidence_quotes.quote: Many video games have found that it is more profitable to not have the consumer purchase a license or a copy of the game, but instead have the base product be free to purchase/play and earn revenue through in-game purchases of cosmetic or other items.
To make the experience more entertaining, these also often show you all the options you could have received to make a player feel like they could've received something better.

This is more akin to a subscription service, where you pay to have access to a service or product for a set period of time, however, these are still sold and advertised as products which you purchase for yourself, and expect to keep access to it til you decide to lose access to it.
the license can be revoked for any reason at any time, often with no warning to the consumer.

paragraphs.text: Digital Fairness is important, but there are still many issues and practices in the way of this. Some examples I will list may be covered or partially covered by this act, but for the sake of completeness, I will list them anyways. I've noticed how gambling or gambling adjacent mechanics have become more and more prevalent in the modern world and a part of online services or products, especially with entertainment. Many video games have found that it is more profitable to not have the consumer purchase a license or a copy of the game, but instead have the base product be free to purchase/play and earn revenue through in-game purchases of cosmetic or other items. Quite often these are sold through loot box mechanis which function similar to slot machines in casinos. To make the experience more entertaining, these also often show you all the options you could have received to make a player feel like they could've received something better. While cosmetics are an interesting way to monetise a game, when created in manner replicating gambling, they often do not have any barrier to entry or warnings attached, to ward away underage kids or people with addictions to gambling. Additionally, many companies are creating games which function essentially identically to gambling style games, most often like slot machines, but skirt around any gambling laws by limiting how you can put money in or receive prizes, however these function the same as a slot machine would. These also have no barrier for entry and often can introduce (and attract) underage persons to gambling. On another topic, in the modern day, where most digital products no longer have a proper physical copy (which does not rely on a network connection to function), most produts are sold as licenses, which often have potentially illegal or malicious terms of service, where the license can be revoked for any reason at any time, often with no warning to the consumer. This is more akin to a subscription service, where you pay to have access to a service or product for a set period of time, however, these are still sold and advertised as products which you purchase for yourself, and expect to keep access to it til you decide to lose access to it. Even if a service is sold as a subscription service, sometimes the service providers can decide to make changes to the product with no warning or consent of the consumer. While it is not unreasonable to expect the provider to have the power to issue changes for the sake of security, stability and improvements, they often can also revoke features or accesses, with no given alternatives. In video games, this is also often an issue, where a game is sold as if a product, but is then revoked or "destroyed" as if a temporary subscription. I believe that the way games are currently sold is not fair to the consumer and should be changed. While there are many ways to achieve this, I will list two ideas below: 1. Require developers or providers to have a clear end of life plan for their product, and be available to the consumer/user/client. This end of life plan should ensure that the developer or provider does their best to leave the product or service in the state it was sold it or give the consumer the ability to achieve this themselves. 2. Set specific laws regarding what can be classified as a sold product, a sold license or what is a subscription. If you're selling a product as a license that can be revoked at any time or does not function without background services, it sounds a lot like a subscription service, such as a movie/video streaming service, except where you just pay a single flat fee. A sold product should be something that the consumer has full access and control over. A subscription wields more control over to the developer or provider. Thank you for your time and consideration!
